A quick message to the listeners. You can find all the necessary images that go along with the narration on our homepage keto forcancer.net. Just click on audiobook. Here you will find the images with the corresponding minutes mark of the chapter. On this homepage, you can also find more information on our donations to Dr. Seaf Freed's research. Thanks for your support. Chapter one, Dr. Thomas Effried, cancer as a metabolic disease on the origin, prevention, and management of cancer. >> All right. So, what I'd like to do to start this off is basically to present a report card on our approach to managing cancer. And as I said, I'm I'm going to speak to you today about cancer in general. and also focus on specific types of cancer in particular uh glyopblasto as kind of an illustrative examples of of our approach to managing the disease. So let's look now these are numbers that we can take from the American Cancer Society and they publish every year data on I guess the war on cancer the success that we're having. So I I compiled just over the last five years. This is 2013 to 2017. And you can see these are pretty sobering numbers. You know, we break them down into new cases, deaths per year, and deaths per day. Simply dividing by 365 give kind of an estimate. And you'll notice that the deaths per day uh and year are exceeding that of the new cases. Um not good. Just to put things into perspective, the population increase in the United States over these same the same period of time was about 2.9%. So um you know how's this war on cancer going? Right? You you look at the numbers, you can make your own decision. These are numbers you don't see on TV, right? You see Opivvo and Katruda and that kind of stuff, but you don't see the the constant increase in in deaths per day. So the question we have to ask ourselves is what's what's going on here? We're not getting success here. This is a failure of of monumental proportions. Right? The numbers, these are large numbers. In China, it's 8,000 over 8,000 a day in China dying from cancer. Cancer has already superseded heart disease in China. So, you know, we go out and we raise money for cancer, right? You all know run, jump. I don't know if you do CrossFit for cancer, but >> sample complete. Ready to continue.

QwYoxdaHNe4.title: From Cancer to Clarity: Metabolic Therapies and the 7 Pillars of Health with Dr Donese Worden
QwYoxdaHNe4.transcript: Welcome to the Superhumaniz podcast. I'm your host, Ariana Summer, and today we're diving into one of the most paradigmshifting conversations in modern medicine, the metabolic theory of cancer. For decades, we've been told that cancer is primarily a genetic disease, a matter of unlucky mutations that require aggressive pharmaceutical interventions. But what if that's only part of the story? What if cancer is fundamentally a metabolic disease, a disorder of cellular energy production that we can address through diet, lifestyle, and targeted metabolic therapies? This isn't fringe science. And it's giving patients new hope and new options beyond the standard cut, poison, and burn model. Today, I'm thrilled to welcome Dr. Denise Warden to the show. Dr. Warden is a boardcertified naturopathic medical doctor, researcher, and global health educator who has dedicated her career to bridging conventional and alternative cancer care. She's collaborated with Banner MD Anderson Cancer Center, served as CEO of Care Oncology, and she's also the creator of the seven pillars of health framework, a comprehensive approach to resilience that addresses everything from sleep and gut health to spiritual well-being. Dr. Warden's motto is educate, empower, enlighten. And that's exactly what we're going to do today. We'll explore the metabolic theory of cancer, discuss both prevention and treatment strategies, and give you a road map for building resilience at every level. >> Super humanize accelerated evolution. Dr. Donise, what a pleasure to connect with you today. I'm really excited for us to have this conversation and yeah, thank you for making time for us. >> Oh, it's amazing. I'm glad to be here. Hope to be of service to everyone. >> Oh, I know you will. That is foundation of your work from what I've learned about you. And that being said, I also want to start at the very foundation. Most of us grew up with the idea that cancer is a genetic disease. It's random mutations in DNA that then go cause cells to go rogue. And actually the entire pharmaceutical industry is built around targeting these mutations. But you and researchers like Dr. Thomas Safe Freed, you argue that this is actually fundamentally backwards and that cancer is primarily a metabolic disease and genetic mutations are downstream effects, not the root cause. Can you break this down for us, Dr. Denise? What does it mean to say cancer is a metabolic disease? >> Let's start with maybe 5% might be genetic, has a big genetic piece, but even if you have a gene, it doesn't mean it needs to express. So, we're back to all the things that you're teaching on your podcast and those of us saying, "Listen, we can do things with our diet, lifestyle, our spirit, our mind and body getting stressed down. So, just because 5% might have a gene, it doesn't need to be expressed necessarily. So, what are the others? What are the others?" And so we go back to Otto Warberg who won the Nobel Prize and it was Nobel Prize for what we're talking about in looking at mitochondrial health and when that damage happens what happens to the cells and is that tied to cancer and our group and Dr. Seed as you mentioned is one of them Dr. Dominant Dagustinino. We have a group of about 80 researchers and physicians and we have to be researchers and physicians to be in this group that we are literally forming right now. The society for international metabolic oncology because the science is pointing in the direction that Otter Worerberg was and that's what on a PET scan when it lights up it's a cancer and what is it lighting up? Why is it lighting up? Because you put sugar into the dye into the system and cancer loves sugar. It's its brain if only fuel and it lights up because it's voracious. So he was right in saying he thought there was a link. Now Dr. Seed and many others are going forward into saying that cancer is a mitochondrial disease. That's where we should be putting our research efforts, our and that's when I work with patients and clients all over the world. We're targeting that with the metabolic therapies, things that we know that are helping the mitochondria of the healthy cells, but also pushing hard against the growth of the cancer by looking at the mitochondria and the metabolic health of the whole person. So, to your point, you did a beautiful job at the beginning. We believe we've gone down the wrong path. Still going down the wrong path. The research dollars are still going into genetics to fund medications or chemotherapies and imunotherapies targeting genetics. But when I look at patients and we look at those values, very few of them have these genetic markers. So then why are we utilizing some of those therapies? But I will say there is a time and a place for chemo, for surgery, and I never thought I'd say this, but there's a time and a place for radiation, for comfort, and for helping. So, we have to be open to all of it. But fundamentally, we've gone down the wrong track in our estimation. >> Yes. And thank you for pointing that out, Dr. Donise. I think it's so important to be open and to look at an individual, to look at the whole individual. what's actually happening here and what you just shared with us about Dr. Otto Warberg and also what's called the Warberg effect. They discovered this for almost a century the mainstream oncology dismissed this as a side effect rather than a cause. And for me it's really it was a revelation when I learned about this and to actually reframe it and see it as the central vulnerability of cancer cells. And I'm curious in your experience from your perspective Dr. Denise, why has it taken so long for this to be taken seriously? And what changed? >> It really changed. It was going down the right direction when Warberg found it and the research was starting to go there. But then the Rockefeller Institute came in. When that happened, homeopathic hospitals, all the what we would call today integrative medicine just got pushed to the side to say we're going to research science-based only. And that's where the pharmaceuticals were developed. So all this got pushed underneath the rug until recently and really the book that Tom Seaff freed wrote Dr. Seaf Freed Metabolic Theory of Cancer opened this back up and another book for readers that want to maybe look into this. It's called Tripping Over the Truth by Travis Kristofferson. As a physician, when I read that book, I said, "That's it. That makes sense. There's the connections. It shows the faults. It shows the history and why." And it's really about the whole industry. It's the billion dollar, billions of dollar industry. Everything is built around this. So we would have to dismantle everything if we said oops that was the wrong thing. But people are starting to look at it now because now younger people are getting cancer. So if it's genetic and we have to wait for those genes to mutate. None of it is making sense. We've got 20 and 30 and 40 year olds. So we know environment is part of it which affects the mitochondria and all the things that we're talking about. But now there's so much and if you look at the data, we really haven't moved the needle much on helping cancer outcomes since we started measuring him in the 1970s. So would you with a business would you say I'm not really getting better but I'm going to keep throwing money into the same thing? we would question it. But it's a very big lift, heavy lift to change this paradigm. And but enough of us with real science, real research, when we can get more funding, we'll do more. But right now, funding monies go typically into those same type of studies >> that are raised in what we consider flawed philosophy of. >> Yes, Dr. Denise. And this is why conversations like these are so important. And I want to recap a little bit of my lay person's understanding if I understand correctly. So the cancer cells have damaged or dysfunctional mitochondria which are the powerhouses of our cells and they become metabolically inflexible. and they become dependent on glutamine and glucose and which is also interesting because this is something we can exploit to our benefit. Right? Before we get into treatment, I would love for you to explain to us what causes this mitochondrial dysfunction in the first place. What damages the mitochondria? You already mentioned its environment, but let's get a deeper understanding on what's actually happening in our world today. You you mentioned younger people are getting cancer. So what's the deal here? >> Sure. >> So we know the environment and those toxins that we're exposed to. We know that damages mitochondria, but also so do antibiotics. We see it. There's ways to try to protect it if someone has to be on an antibiotic. But how many times have your listeners been prescribed an antibiotic and a doctor gave them talked to them about NAC or other things that can help protect from that damage? We just it's not part of the paradigm yet. But I would say stress also does when we're worrying. Some of the stress most stressed people I know are biohackers because they're so busy trying to do all the right things all the time and that they're stressed on trying to what's my next stack? What's my next thing to do? And we have to remember back to what you talked about, there's two real pathways that feed cancer cells. Okay? One is the glucose, which you mentioned. We can do a lot with that. We can say we got to change the diet. We put most of our patients on a ketogenic diet. Highfat, low protein, not Atkins, not high protein. You have to do it right if you're going to do it. But we do that. But they can still have trouble getting into ketosis. And because when we're stressed, we're going to pull it out of the muscles. We're going to pull it from other places and still dump glucose into the system. So that brings us back to the stress side. We have to address that. The other big pathway is that glutamine, glutamate that you're talking about on this side. One of the big feeders of that pathway again is stress. We're trying to find the best blockers we can for both of those pathways. Some are repurposed drugs. This is not all natural medicine that we're talking about. We've got different molecules we're looking at now that are looking good on trying to blot some of these pathways, but a lot of it for those that are healthy at the moment and saying, "I don't want to get cancer." There's things to think about. But I always go back to the top. If you're not addressing the stress, no matter how good we are at these other pathways of blocking them, doing all the ketogenic diet, hyperbaric oxygen, the treatments I'm sure you're going to want to talk about in a minute. All of that we can only get so far if we're still dumping sugar from the system, those adrenal glands running cortisol or pulling from the muscles because you're telling the body it's in stress and it needs to survive. It's going to choose survival in the moment over I need to fight this cancer. >> Yes, Dr. Donise. And to circle back real quick to something you said just a few minutes ago when you talked about getting the patients on the ketogenic diet, highfat, low protein. I know from conversations with friends when this comes up and especially the ones who are quite focused on a diet that is rich in animal proteins, they're like, "Why low protein? Can you break it down for us, please?" Be able to get into ketosis. That means that your body is burning fat and it's making a different fuel. Instead of the brain and the body using glucose, now it's using ketones. By the way, those are far better, superior, and cleaner fuel for the brain. That's why it works in neurological diseases, Parkinson's, MS. We're learning more and more. So, it's a clean fuel and to be able to get there, for the body to produce that certain things will interfere and high protein can interfere. Now, everyone's a little different. We put them on chronometer and things to be able to say how much protein can we have because one of the ways that cancer can kill you is sarcopenia which is the wasting of the muscles. So we have to weigh this. Sometimes I might say okay we're going to sacrifice being so deep into ketosis because we need more protein to support the muscles. So it's always an individualized personalized program that should be going on because we're all different. But the general is higher fat, lower protein, which doesn't mean low protein. It means lower than the fat. And the carbs are really coming from vegetables because we're really trying to get rid of the fruits and you might be able to have a handful of berries. >> We're looking at their CGMs. We're looking at their glucose. And we're saying, "Okay, you can handle this. You can handle that. You're in ketosis. You're doing well. Let's tweak it." Because we've got to make sure good nutrition is going too. And a lot of people that say, "I'm in ketosis," they're not doing it right. They're not measuring. They're not looking at what they need to. And so, even if it's just for weight loss, you need to do it right or it's just another diet. It's just another stress and the weight will come back anyway. So, it's got to be individualized. >> Yes, for sure. Dr. Donise. on knowing that it needs to be individualized. Are there some suggestions you can share with us or are there certain huristics for example with regard to measuring what are your preferred methods? >> Yeah, right now there's a device called Keto Mojo and because it's looking at the glucose and the gl it's looking at different parameters and what we do is calculate what's called GKI. Who came up with that was Dr. Tom Seaffrey because now we're looking at the glutamate, the glucose. We're looking at the a bigger picture than just are you producing ketones and sometimes people can't get into ketosis because their blood sugar is too high. Sometimes they're not getting there and we have to figure out why because their glucose is low. So the GKI is a great way to measure it. The urine strips, forget it. Don't do it. They're not measurable. It's not accurate. That was the only thing we had to begin with. We have some that you can blow into once you understand and know what you're doing. The bio sense, others that you can say, am I in ketosis? Those are fairly accurate. You can use just breath blowing, but you can't do that at the beginning. We don't think it's the best. We really like looking for this GKI number until we get everything figured out, till we know the diet, we know the stress, we've got it all, everybody's doing well, and then still we're going to want to look at that once in a while. But and again we've got to say are we causing more stress by measuring all this all the time. >> So at the beginning I tell the patients look listen give me a couple week look at it that we're going to measure we're going to look just relax let's breathe let's do the things that I'm trying to teach them to do to help them with their stress but hang with me until we nail this down. Then you get to really relax because you're doing the best that you can instead of I hope this works or I hope that works and let me just do everything that I just heard on social media or I read about let me just do it all which is we all know is not the right way to do it. >> No. And speaking about stress, can you share some of your preferred protocols or methods? And again, this will be tailor made, of course, to the individual you're with, but what have you found can really >> very quickly relieve people of stress and especially if it's people who are used to that state of being stressed. >> That's right. They don't even know they're stressed because they haven't felt this peaceful, parasympathetic, calm state in a while. They think it's normal. Their brain waves are up here really high and fast and incoherent. They're just a mess and they're going to sleep like that. And even if they can stay asleep, they're not getting good quality sleep because the brain's still doing this. They're running from the bear and they're asleep. So the first is recognition of that there is stress, what it's doing. I might need to show them a cortisol. Some people need to see labs. They need to see data to go, "Oh, okay. That's not good. Oh, my glucose is high. I'm not getting it down with all these things I'm doing. It might be because I'm stressing. So, you have to Some people are just ready and they go just tell me what to do. And others we have to go slowly. Now, my favorites are meditation with music because most people if we say just go meditate and quiet the brain, they're going to feel like a miserable failure because they're just they can't quiet that brain down. It's just too hard for most at the beginning. But at the if we can teach them active meditation, it's breath work where they breathe in, they hold, breathe in, they hold. And they've been taught what this is doing to their body. What is it? What's the mechanics behind it and the science behind it and they go, "Okay, that makes sense." And teaching them to breathe, teaching them to meditate. And I will tell you that music from what I have seen, I've seen it in real time, is the fastest way to shift you from one emotional state to the next. And music that's been prepared where it's in training the heart and the brain. It's slowing them down. It's helping them be more coherent and match one another. It's very quick on EEGs with the right kind of music. It immediately makes a shift. Then they're able to get into a meditative state. Sometimes with breath work, sometimes just listening to guided. We're all different. We all like different things. But I like to switch it up. But I by the end of a visit with a patient, I usually know what type of stress reduction I could do. It might just need to be journaling because there's so religious belief won't even let them say the word meditation. >> Even though I say, "Okay, let's talk about breath work. Is that okay? No, that's woo too. Okay, >> how about journaling? You have to take each person >> and then just and I will work with them with breath work just in the moment and say now how do you feel >> after doing that and they'll go oh wow wow >> okay they just don't know >> your audience is well educated you what they hear from you and what they're doing they get it so it's not one form is better than another which one works for you and music is my favorite and then you add on meditation breath whatever it is that you need to do. >> Yes, thank you for sharing that, Dr. Donise. And I agree, music is amazing. It can happen literally within seconds. And breath, it's for free. We have access to it. I just last weekend participated in a somatic breath work >> session uh of a friend of mine and it was incredible. It was a group of women and literally the release one woman was literally whailing and keening and bless her for being able to release what she's carrying and thank her for being a permission slip to the rest of us women as well. But that's the power of breath for you. >> Yes. >> And so I'm also interested in speaking about fats and a high fat diet. What are your go-tos? What are your preferred fats? First, I do a food sensitivity test to see what foods they're allergic to. So, if I'm going to ask them to eat a lot of something, I first need to make sure it's not causing inflammation in their body. But let's assume that we've looked at that and that it's all okay. Avocados, all the good oils, olive oil, and oils that if they're not cooked, even olive oil, those are healthy. We put them on the food after they're cooked. If you're cooking, you're better off with lard and other things. This gets into the whole oil issue, but clean oils, those that will take high heat if you're cooking with it. But for us, for our purposes, we need things. It could be oily fish, salmon, all the things that have good naturally occurring omega-3s. So, it's the good oils, coconut oil, avocado oil, the walnut oils, the nut oils, all those kinds of oils. And again, they're better not heated, but avocados, salmon, and then they can they you can look it up nowadays with AI. What are the healthiest highfat foods that you like be able to look at it and then we really analyze, are you getting enough of the what we call the macronutrients, right? xd7JVZJx_bU.title: The Origin of the Ketogenic Diet - COMPLETE AUDIOBOOK. (By Dom D'Agostino & Travis Christofferson)
xd7JVZJx_bU.transcript: the origin and future of the ketogenic diet by Dr Dominic D'Agostino and Travis Christopherson read by Johnny rockermeyer chapter one Jim Abrahams and his newborn son Charlie the year is 1992. in the front yard on an innocent spring morning in 1993 Hollywood movie producer Jim Abrahams rhythmically pushed his son Charlie in a swing behind him were the busy noises of his wife Nancy and Charlie's two siblings as they prepared the house for a celebration it was Charlie's first birthday nobody was prepared for what came next it happened in a moment suddenly Charlie's head slumped to the side and then out of nowhere his arm shot into the air as if possessed by some unseen Force deep down Abrahams knew that something was seriously wrong a moment before his son was healthy and now a fleeting instant later he was not a series of neurological tests proved his instincts were correct your son has epilepsy the neurologist told him he then went on to explain that epilepsy although a single diagnosis really consists of a wide spectrum of illness from mild to severe and within the Spectrum the seizures that Define the disease are as diverse as a box of crayons partial generalized and absent seizures comprise the three main groupings these are then further parsed into tonic clonic tonic clonic myoclonic anatomic seizures each category describes a choreographed path of involuntary muscle contractions that spin outward from the neurological chaos unraveling within the brain as some time passed tragically Charlie's case landed more toward the severe end of the spectrum and it wasn't just the nature of the seizures it was the frequency he was having up to 100 seizures a day it was heartbreaking to watch Time After Time the toddler's brain was taken over while he tried to play in the middle of stacking a block or blowing a horn he would pause and then slump over sometimes remaining still and others with a limb or two shaking uncontrollably and then it was over and life would more or less resume where it left off it was life-lived in intervals a day was not the seamless Continuum most of us take for granted it was a chopped up into tiny little slices it was a fate worse than death said Abrahams the house was filled with tears all of us all the time cried Charlie was put on one medication after another a process that seemed haphazard and experimental the odds were already stacked against them if an epileptic child fails the first drug the chance the next drug will work drops to 10 to 15 percent nevertheless the doses were upped and the drugs were given in combination new with old old with new but nothing seemed to blunt the attacks worse the Abrahams is watched helplessly as the drugs changed their child in the few good moments he was spared you pour the drugs down your child's throat despite the fact there is something inside you that says wait a minute this can't be right said Abrahams my son was so loaded at times he just lived in his car seat he was essentially non-functional the abrahamses were delicately told by the doctors that Charlie's illness came with additional consequences the severity and frequency at this age would affect the development of his brain if left unchecked it would lead to Progressive retardation so it was a Race Against Time desperate the abrahamses did everything they could The Physician they went to at UCLA Dr Donald Shields was a nationally recognized expert in children's epilepsy they didn't stop there they sought a second third fourth and fifth opinion but nothing new was offered each doctor's opinion was just a reiteration of the last stick with the drugs but the drugs simply weren't working the doctors didn't seem to share the Abrahams sense of urgency every day was important Charlie was slowly falling into an abyss the doctors finally capitulated and they turned to the option held in reserve surgery but the glimmer of hope surgery held vanished once Charlie recovered and the Relentless assault of seizures continued with nowhere left to turn at the end of the road the Abrahams is turned to a faith healer still nothing for the abrahamses if any sort of resignation creeped in it only served to stoke a fire there is no greater instinctual surge than of protective parents after thousands of epileptic seizures an incredible number of drugs dozens of blood draws eight hospitalizations a mountain of eegs MRIs CT scans and pet scans one fruitless brain surgery five pediatric neurologists in three cities two homeopaths one faith healer and countless prayers the seizures continued unchecked but giving up was never an option and so Jim Abrahams began his own research he began combing through books and attending medical lectures it was an education rolled up in a frantic mission then one day on the bottom shelf at the medical Library he picked up a book published that year by Dr John Freeman titled seizures and epilepsy in childhood a guide for parents the book was written for parents to help them cope but buried deep inside were three pages on a dietary protocol used to treat epilepsy Dr Freeman had tried to write a book focused solely on the diet but was unable to find a publisher so he snuck them into this book almost as an afterthought within the few pages was the remarkable claim that the diet was able to help at least half of those who maintained it the first impulse that ran through Abraham's mind was bewilderment if it had any legitimacy why hadn't one of the five doctors mention this treatment option by now I couldn't make the leap between all these doctors saying one thing and this other guy saying something else he later confessed confused and desperate he had what he felt were only two options left the strange diet and an herbalist someone had told him about working out of a strip mall in Houston Texas at the next appointment when he asked Dr Shields which one he should pursue Shields said flip a coin neither will probably work and so he did just that he and his wife Nancy literally flipped a coin heads the herbalist Tales the strange diet it landed on heads so the family traveled to Houston and met the herbalist they took his advice bought a grocery bag full of herbs and returned home after a few weeks of mixing herbal teas it became apparent that the strange smelling concoctions were having no effect on Charlie's seizures this was the end of the line the best doctors in the world the best drugs in the world surgery homeopaths Faith healers herbalists everything had failed Charlie Charlie and his family were backed into a corner there was only one option left so with only a thread of Hope desperate deflated and exhausted the abrahamses boarded a plane to Baltimore Maryland to see John Freeman about the peculiar and obscure treatment called the ketogenic diet oddly enough as they flew to the east coast at some point in the Upper Midwest perhaps over the Ohio Valley if the abrahamses could have peered 80 years into the past they would have looked down and seen a train heading in the opposite direction to Battle Creek Michigan on board were their exact counterparts A desperate family with their epileptic Sun at the end of their rope traveling to the only option left to them a long shot Mark Twain said history doesn't repeat itself but it does rhyme and the stories of these families rhymed in every sentence and verse but in a larger context each story astonishing in its own right serves only as bookmarks it is what happened in the interim the 80 years that lie in between which reveals volumes critically it is a vitally important lesson of the infallibility and undulating path of Medical Science and a stark reminder that valuable knowledge even in the modern era can be lost the story of both families Converge on a single man Bernard McFadden although almost entirely forgotten today in the early 1900s almost everyone knew his name McFadden was a Bonafide American icon his rise to fame was also uniquely American he started with nothing in fact less than nothing he was cast into the world with the odds decidedly stacked against him his early years were filled with pain and abandonment his father beat him and his family before drinking himself to death when McFadden was five years old when Bernard McFadden was seven his mother sent him away to the cheapest boarding school she could find because she was dying from late stage tuberculosis and was unable to care for him any longer McFadden then escaped what he called the starvation school and fell into a series of indentured servitudes for distant relatives and Farmers working 100-hour weeks and paid only in room and board but like all American icons McFadden fought back scratching and clawing for a better place in the world when he was 15 he accidentally wandered into a newly established gymnasium in St Louis one looked past the threshold was all it took inside men were grunting and sweating while they hoisting dumbbells and pre-formed calisthenics posters of muscle men plastered the walls as he stared open-mouthed and wide-eyed McFadden was at the same time enthralled and infected right there he swore an oath I'm going to look like them I'm going to be like them over time Bernard McFadden parlayed his sworn oath into an Empire recognizing an opportunity he started a magazine called physical culture what started as a modest Enterprise soon rocketed Skyward with dazzling growth it was a fresh new message for Americans within the pages he proselytized Health vitality and uninhibited enjoyment of the human body he advocated exercise healthy eating and avoidance of tobacco too much alcohol and white bread a substance McFadden called the staff of death he encouraged nude sunbathing walking barefoot and warned of the evils of prudishness the body claimed McFadden was for unrestrained enjoyment for the first time Americans were told sex was good and was nothing to be ashamed of his magazines were provocative the first to use scantily clad models engaging and told Americans there was a new and better way to live he had tapped into a new Niche he had become America's First Health Guru forging a new culture and selling a lifestyle he was the driving force behind the rise of bodybuilding he hand-picked an unknown Brooklyn model named Charles Atlas and gave him the title world's most perfect man and then was the architect behind a career path that led to atlas's fame and fortune Americans appetite for McFadden's new lifestyle was insatiable halfway through the year 1900 McFadden was selling 110 000 copies of his magazine physical culture per month his rapid success came with a new cockiness the wildly popular magazine claimed McFadden not only blazed the path to health and vitality now it was revealing his secrets to curing disease any disease his belief was sincere and consisted of a prescriptive lifestyle that he claimed would bring anyone back from the throes of illness the prescription consisted of the principles he had been proselytizing for most of his life exercise sunlight avoidance of Alcohol Tobacco and diet but the most important piece the one imperative ingredient to pull people from the ghostly Realm of the sick back into Vitality was something McFadden felt held almost magical healing properties fasting McFadden's belief in fasting while may be largely forgotten certainly wasn't new its restorative powers go far back into antiquity the Greek physician Hippocrates made reference to the healing properties of fasting he said everyone has a doctor in him we just have to help him in his work the natural healing Force within each one of us is the greatest force in getting well to eat when you are sick is to feed your sickness incredibly the virtues of fasting or over consumption in general went even further back all the way to 3800 BC an inscription found in an Egyptian pyramid Reed humans live on one quarter of what they eat on the other three quarters lives their doctor a glib quote that could easily describe the relationship between food and medicine today other famous people have touted fasting throughout history including Ben Franklin the best of all medicines is resting and fasting Mark Twain wrote a little starvation can really do more for the average sick man than can the best medicines and the best doctors McFadden embraced the time-honored practice that had strangely bounced in and out of favor through thousands of years of History perhaps the counter-intuitive nature of sick people not eating served to pull it back Into Obscurity time and time again rarely would a grandmother parent or a physician in their right mind suggest that a sick person should stop eating it goes against every fiber of instinct however Bernard McFadden stumbled onto the salubrious practice of fasting his faith in its powers were reinforced by personal observation and trial while working on a farm he had noticed that whenever an animal became ill it stopped eating years later one spring he felt the early symptoms of pneumonia stirring in his chest he remembered the lesson of the farm animals and cut back to a couple pieces of fruit per day he noticed by the second day his chest had begun to clear and by the fourth day the symptoms had all but vanished McFadden's sideways entry into medicine ultimately culminated in the creation of a sanatorium in 1907. a massive Mansion equipped with swimming pools gyms Russian and Turkish baths and relaxation areas the Berner McFadden sanatorium he had changed his name from Bernard to Berner because it sounded like the Roar of a lion was located in Battle Creek Michigan a small town turned overnight into a beacon for the sick directly across the street stood the famous Battle Creek sanitarium started by John Harvey Kellogg whose brother capitalized on the health Trend and started the Kellogg cereal company 300 000 Health Seekers visited the Battle Creek sanitarium during its 65 years of operation every day the train dropped off a fresh crop complaining of a variety of ailments a portion of which now headed to McFadden's new sanatorium with the hope of getting well most of those that showed up on McFadden's doorstep had vague symptoms like headaches weight loss digestive problems it was really no surprise as the typical diet of a century ago consisted mostly of salted pork bread and potatoes the first vitamin would not be discovered until 1912 and Americans were entirely unaware how deficient their diets truly were also the workforce had begun the transition to desk jobs the combination of a terrible diet and an activity left many Americans in a sorry state so they turned to McFadden to help at the sanatorium McFadden hired an osteopath doctor named Hugh Conklin as an assistant he prescribed to the same belief system as McFadden they both believed that medicine the way it was practiced was largely fraudulent together they called out the medical establishment we will take those you have given up on and cure them this was fine for the mostly vague and undiagnosable masses that showed up after a period of stress-free fasting hydrotherapy and light exercise in and around the beautiful mansion they did feel better of course some of the more conservative doctors pushed back claiming McFadden and his hand-picked doctors were the worst kind of quacks the kind that preyed on those that were healthy but thought they were sick but McFadden had star power on his side one of his more famous clients Upton Sinclair defended him vigorously even publishing a book titled the fasting cure dedicated to his good friend BM occasionally patients with very tangible illnesses like epilepsy showed up on McFadden's doorstep for the detractors of McFadden this they thought would surely be where he would fail epileptic patients in particular came with a built-in yardstick the therapies prescribed would face a true test because of one simple fact the number of seizures could be counted before and after treatment thus determining if the treatment was effective or not it's not clear if Conklin or McFadden knew the history of epilepsy if they did they would have known there was a scattering of evidence backing the idea that fasting would actually work for epilepsy in the 5th Century BC Hippocrates reported on a man who had been seized by convulsions a fast was prescribed and the Cure worked freeing the individual of seizures in a quotation from the King James version of the Bible Mark tells the story of Jesus curing an epileptic boy where his disciples had failed when they asked him why Jesus said this kind can come out by nothing but prayer and fasting and so Conklin began to count he recorded the number of seizures before and after treatment when the numbers were tallied the result was shocking and undeniable fasting did work for epileptic patients once they stopped eating an invisible metabolic Levy was raised around the brain the neurological storms that once pounded the shores were abruptly dispelled for some reason however Conklin felt no immediate compulsion to publish his data but it didn't matter nature abhors a vacuum the medical establishment had no good Solutions and so word of the drugless Healer in Battle Creek Michigan claiming to have an effective treatment for epilepsy gradually began filtering into the public at large as rumors of Conklin's water diet rippled outward from the center of the country doctors continued to treat epilepsy with the few drugs they had the most commonly prescribed drug was potassium bromide in the mid-1800s it was thought epilepsy was caused by excessive sexual Indulgence particularly masturbation in 1857 British Dr Charles Lockhart read a report claiming people had become temporarily impotent after taking potassium bromide by his own Reckoning Lockhart made the connection maybe the drug would assuage the sexual gluttony of epileptics and as a result stop their seizures the drug worked in spite of Lockhart's faulty logic a decade later doctors began questioning the link between excessive sexuality and epilepsy more likely they reasoned bromide was acting directly on the brain rather than through the indirect suppression of sexual desire bromide is a powerful sedative and researchers made the irresistible connection between the nervous eruption of a seizure and the Drug's ability to slow nervous impulses like pouring water on a fire in 1872 one researcher wrote the object of medical treatment for epilepsy is to control the over-readiness of nervous action for this purpose sedatives have been employed with success the connection was powerfully seductive and would guide the search for new anti-seizure medications for the foreseeable future the search for new epilepsy drugs was now synonymous to finding drugs that were sedative the two qualities were thought to be inseparable the next breakthrough happened on a fall day in 1912. it happened because a young medical student named Alfred halpman was sleep deprived the young German resident psychiatrist lived over the epileptic Ward while he was in medical training below him seizures tormented his patients throughout the night he could hear them thrash and convulse sometimes even the thud of one of them falling out of bed The unremitting Sounds forbade sleep as he tossed and turned he realized he had to try something a new sleeping pill called luminal had just hit the pharmacy a year earlier during his medical training he learned potassium bromide worked as an anti-seizure medication precisely because it was a sedative it wasn't unreasonable to think that luminal might work through the same mechanism in the very least he thought maybe both he and his patients could get a good night's sleep and so the next evening right before bedtime he gave each of his patients a dose of the new drug to his Delight hoppedman slept soundly that night as did his patience he would have left it at that a simple remedy for a good night's sleep but he noticed something remarkable his patients reprieve from seizures extended into the next day they didn't have any hoppedman made the connection hovering before him perhaps this drug chemically known as phenobarbital could be a new medication for epilepsy once he published his observation phenobarbital began to be prescribed and slowly built momentum eventually overtaking potassium bromide's position as the first line treatment for epilepsy although better at preventing seizures than potassium bromide for patients luminal was still a compromise fewer seizures was swapped for a life lived in a fog a hazy blunted state of existence doctors assumed it was a zero-sum game fewer seizures came at the expense of sedation it was accepted this was just the way it had to be chapter 2. Charles howland's question in 1921 H wrong guyland a prominent endocrinologist at New York Presbyterian Hospital took the podium at the annual American Medical Association Convention rather than the typical dry run through mountains of grass and charts guyland chose to tell a story he spoke of a young cousin who had epilepsy over four years he watched as his cousin tried every treatment recommended by several neurologists including the boy's Uncle who was a professor of pediatrics at Johns Hopkins the family watched helpless as every treatment failed including bromines and the newer drug phenobarbital luminal the desperate family took what they saw as the only option left a train ride to Battle Creek Michigan to see McFadden and Conklin by now Conklin had treated an Untold number of patients probably numbering in the hundreds guylin explained how the young cousin fasted under the supervision of Conklin for three or four weeks remarkably the seizures stopped after the second day and the remission proved remarkably durable his cousin remained seizure-free for over two years after completion of the treatment for guylin it was impossible to ignore the result first because of the intimate family connection and second because he knew his cousin had failed every other treatment the best those in the audience the doctors sitting before him had to offer so guylen looked closer he carefully followed two other patients that traveled to Battle Creek to see Dr Conklin the patience guylin confirmed at least for the time being were cured they remained free of seizures after returning home then Rog Island took the next logical step he saw if he could replicate Conklin's results by fasting his own patients now in front of the hushed audience he presented the results after fasting 30 patients for 20 days in his Clinic 87 percent of the patients became seizure free the results evoked gasps from the audience the rumors of the drugless midwestern healer that publicly announced his disdain for conventional medicine were now staring them in the face it was no longer a rumor guyland had casted into documented fact for the doctors in the auditorium the treatment of epilepsy remained as frustrating of an Enterprise as it always had surely patients with no other disease have grasped at so many therapeutic straws wrote One doctor epilepsy comes from ancient Greece meaning to seize possess or afflict the Greeks also called epilepsy the sacred disease and as civilizations before them they thought it was a form of spiritual possession consequently for centuries victims were treated with one pointless ritual after the next in the 5th Century BC Hippocrates boldly challenged the Assumption epilepsy was divine in origin declaring it was a medically treatable problem emanating from the brain Hippocrates declaration would change the course of the disease forever thrusting it into the empirical Arena of real medicine but the transition would not be smooth his declaration started what would be a series of clumsy barbaric and sometimes bizarre attempts to treat the disease The Eccentric and unrelated treatments were a reflection of the fact epilepsy was of mysterious and Unknown Origin doctor's attempts to mute epileptic fits were Patchwork of trial and error bloodletting trefening of the skull which is boring a hole in the skull to release the disease removal of the ovaries and adrenal glands countless drugs herbs and tinctures when the Roman physician celsus witnessed epileptics drinking blood from the wounds of dying Gladiators he wrote what a miserable disease that makes tolerable such a miserable remedy and in 1921 as guyland gave his presentation little had changed many modern cures are not less miserable wrote a respected neurologist comparing the state of treatment in the 1920s to those of the past doctors still have little to offer the victims of epilepsy and that a non-conventional osteopath had discovered what might be the best Treatment available was met with a degree of skepticism frustration and embarrassment the father of guyland's young cousin Charles Howland was a wealthy New York corporate attorney shocked that the cure to his son's epilepsy lies so far outside the medical establishment Howland became obsessed with a single question why did fasting cure his son of epilepsy Conklin already thought he knew the answer he claimed epilepsy emanated from the intestines he speculated that toxins were secreted from the lymph nodes surrounding the small intestine then stored in the lymph system and from time to time discharged into the bloodstream causing seizures simply not eating allowed the toxins to be cleared Conklin had no evidence to back his claim his reasoning was nothing more than a wild guess probably pushed Along by McFadden along with many doctors Charles Howland wasn't satisfied with Conklin's contrived explanation instinctually Howland felt there was more to it he desperately wanted to know he wanted an answer backed by actual evidence in Hope of finding an answer he wrote a check to his brother a professor of pediatrics at Johns Hopkins for five thousand dollars five thousand dollars went a long way in the early 1900s and Dr Howland used the money to set up a state-of-the-art laboratory at Johns Hopkins dedicated to the new mission as with his brother the question quickly became a particular obsession the answer reasoned Howland was sure to lie within some shift in the epileptics metabolism to find the answer would be an exercise in comparison what did the metabolism of the epileptic look like before and after fasting and could the relevant Factor be isolated from the noise far too steep a task for any individual he turned to Dr James gamble an unusually precise and methodical clinical chemist the canvas for gamble search consisted of four fasting epileptic children he carefully monitored every known biochemical variable as they transitioned into the fasting state he collected and exhaustively analyzed their urine and blood with painstaking detail from water loss to electrolytes balance acid-base balance and the Curious mention of the strange occurrence of two ketones Beta hydroxybutyrate And acetoacetate in the fasting patient's plasma and urine to gamble the compounds were a mystery he speculated that they were meaningless the byproduct of the incomplete oxidation of fats nothing more than a useless exhaust expelled as the patients began to burn fat in the end despite an extensive search the report held no definitive answer the biochemical shift that reduced seizures in fasting epileptics for the time being remained a mystery as howland's team continued its frenzied search about a thousand miles west in Chicago the seeds were being planted to offer up a different explanation chapter 3. food becomes medicine Roland woodyatt and Everts Graham both medical doctors in Chicago were having an argument over lunch the waiter was already tense their voices were elevated and worse they had scribbled Arcane symbols all over the tablecloth it was most likely ruined the argument had drifted away from the scribblings into something more personal your perfectionism is holding you back can't you see that said Graham clearly frustrated did you know it took Lorenzo gilberti 20 years just to sculpt the bronze doors of the baptistry of sangiovanni Perfection doesn't care how long something takes retorted Woody at in a measured voice ATT was a perfectionist in every sense of the word and Graham was not the first to notice he could agonize over the wording of a single sentence sometimes for an entire day Graham found this absurd a waste of time and talent but for the study of human metabolism woodyat's peculiar personality trait was perhaps his greatest strength he was intensely passionate about the details he could zero in focus with singular purpose and not allow himself to be railroaded by the incredibly complex Maze of metabolic pathways where others quickly became overwhelmed in the summer of 1921 woodyat's passion for metabolism culminated in an article titled objects and method of diet adjustment in diabetes although insulin had yet to be isolated researchers knew that the pancreas was the site of pathology injecting diabetic dogs with pancreatic extract could normalize blood sugar levels would he at realized that the problem in the diabetic lies solely in pancreatic dysfunction resulting in an inability to utilize excessive carbohydrate at the time Physicians often fasted diabetics until glucose disappeared from the urine the idea being to rest the pancreas a normal diet was then slowly reintroduced usually with carbohydrates first unfortunately the diabetics soon found themselves back where they started with sugar building up to malevolent levels within the bloodstream that fasting cleared the bloodstream of sugar made Woody at curious inspiring him to ask a simple question if fasting diabetic patients resort to burning their own fat why not just provide fat through diet keep the carbohydrates away and keep the diabetic in the fasted State indefinitely it was a simple proposal of exclusion it was a question that perhaps should have been asked sooner but at the time it was easy for researchers to get lost in the details big questions still lingered the largest was the fate of dietary fat it was not clear if fat could be converted directly into sugar but woodyat refused to let the gaps in knowledge lead to a dead end when a pathway was unknown he let empirical macro evidence guide him in this case he knew another group had already experimented with a high-fat low-carb diet in diabetics with striking success even if fat was able to be converted into glucose it really didn't matter reasoned Woody at something appeared to be blocking its conversion he was formulating a hypothesis that had only lingered in and out of researchers minds but had never been concretely suggested once he wrote it down it seemed obvious why not shift the ratio of the diet in favor of fat this way the diabetic would be able to rest his pancreas remove the excess sugar from his bloodstream and utilize fat instead as an energy source the smoldering problems of insulin carbohydrates and blood sugar were removed from the equation he couldn't help chastising the medical community for their inflexible dogmatic assumptions isolating the problem as a tendency toward dietary group think the universal custom of thinking of the food supply simply as so much carbohydrate so much protein so much fat and so many calories without further analysis wrote wittyat the patient doesn't even have to be deprived he reasoned they could consume the same amount of total calories in a single sentence woodyat toppled the monolithic view of diet now because of him diet was no longer viewed as a concrete pillar now it was a column built from subcategories carbohydrate protein and fat that could be manipulated shifted and restacked in different combinations depending on the needs of the patient while woodyat was exposing fissures in dietary preconceptions in the summer of 1921 350 miles Northeast of Chicago at the Mayo Clinic in Rochester Minnesota a doctor named Russell Wilder published three short paragraphs in the clinical Bulletin the letter described the same dietary Epiphany as woodyat maintaining the fasting state by replacing carbohydrates with fat but Wilder imagine treating a different disease epilepsy it has occurred to
